coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots recovery from waste streams of hydrothermal process
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lin
Zhang, Jing
Zhao, Siyu
Feng, Chao
Lv, Yanan
Wei, Zicong
Zhao, Peitao
Song, Xueping
coredata.dc:description: Waste streams from hydrothermal process typically contain high levels of aldehydes, volatile fatty acids, phenols, 5-hydroxymethyl furfural, and some dissolved organic compounds that could contaminate water bodies and soils. To address the problems of high organic content and high disposal costs in waste streams generated in hydrothermal process. This work proposes a process for the recovery and extraction of carbon dots (CDs) from hydrothermal waste streams and a new idea for the preparation of CDs by hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) of high-ash biomass with polyvinyl chloride (PVC). The HTC of corncob (CC), corn stalk (CS), and the co-HTC of PVC with CC, CS waste streams were used as raw materials for the extraction and recovery of CDs. The results showed that the CDs obtained from the HTC of CC, CS (CC-CDs, CS-CDs) were more homogeneous in particle size and distribution and had more hydrophilic functional groups on the surface. Whereas the CDs obtained from the co-HTC of PVC with CC, CS (PCC-CDs, PCS-CDs) were of different particle sizes and had an aggregated distribution. In addition, the incorporation of PVC improved the excitation wavelength dependence of CDs. The relative percentage of Si2p in the PCS-CDs is much higher than that in the other CDs, with higher fluorescence quantum yields. Based on these results, the feasibility of the process for extracting CDs from hydrothermal waste streams was evaluated, providing theoretical guidance for extracting valuable products from hydrothermal waste streams and compensating the cost of wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Polyvinyl chloride
Waste streams
Hydrothermal process
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of the effect of PVC on the properties of CDs in co-HTC

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research received funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51906079), the Foundation of Xuzhou Science and Technology Program (KC23356), the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University (2021KF20), the Foundation of Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (SJCX23-1323), and the Foundation of Graduate Innovation Program of China University of Mining and Technology (2023WLJCRCZL205).

This research received funding from the Xuzhou Science and Technology Program (KC23356), the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control (2021KF20), the Foundation of National Natural Science Foundation of China (51906079). The above funded countries are all China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, HR-TEM, and particle size distribution statistics of CDs: (a1-a3): CC-CDs, (b1-b3): CS-CDs, (c1-c3): PCC-CDs, and (d1-d2): PCS-CDs. CDs: carbon dots; CC-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of corncob; CS-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of corn stalk; PCC-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the co-hydrothermal carbonization of polyvinyl chloride and corncob; PCS-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the co-hydrothermal carbonization of polyvinyl chloride and corn stalk.

XRD pattern for (a) CC-CDs, (b) CS-CDs, (c) PCC-CDs, and (d) PCS-CDs. CDs: carbon dots; CC-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of corncob; CS-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of corn stalk; PCC-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the co-hydrothermal carbonization of polyvinyl chloride and corncob; PCS-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the co-hydrothermal carbonization of polyvinyl chloride and corn stalk.

FTIR spectrum of CDs. CDs: carbon dots; CC-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of corncob; CS-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of corn stalk; PCC-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the co-hydrothermal carbonization of polyvinyl chloride and corncob; PCS-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the co-hydrothermal carbonization of polyvinyl chloride and corn stalk.

C1s, and O1s high-resolution XPS spectra of spectra for (a–c) CC-CDs; (d–f) CS-CDs; (g–i) PCC-CDs; and (j–l) PCS-CDs, respectively. CDs: carbon dots; CC-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of corncob; CS-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of corn stalk; PCC-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the co-hydrothermal carbonization of polyvinyl chloride and corncob; PCS-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the co-hydrothermal carbonization of polyvinyl chloride and corn stalk.

UV–vis absorption spectrum and the digital image of the CDs under 365 nm UV light from (a) CC-CDs, (b) CS-CDs, (c) PCC-CDs, and (d) PCS-CDs. CDs: carbon dots; CC-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of corncob; CS-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of corn stalk; PCC-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the co-hydrothermal carbonization of polyvinyl chloride and corncob; PCS-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the co-hydrothermal carbonization of polyvinyl chloride and corn stalk.

PL emission spectrum obtained from different excitation wavelengths of (a) CC-CDs, (b) CS-CDs, (c) PCC-CDs, and (d) PCS-CDs. PL: photoluminescence; CDs: carbon dots; CC-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of corncob; CS-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of corn stalk; PCC-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the co-hydrothermal carbonization of polyvinyl chloride and corncob; PCS-CDs: carbon dots obtained from the co-hydrothermal carbonization of polyvinyl chloride and corn stalk.
Atomic concentration of C, N, O, Cl, and Si in XPS. C: carbon; N: nitrogen; O: oxygen; Cl: chlorine; Si: silicon.
The proximate and ultimate analysis of raw materials and hydrochars [30].

row.entry: Ultimate analysis c (wt.%, dry ash-free basis)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: VM: volatile matter, FC: fixed carbon.

Note: HC-CC and HC-CS represent hydrochars derived from corncob and corn stalk, respectively. HC-PCC and HC-PCS represent co-hydrochars derived from corncob and corn stalk after co-hydrothermal carbonization with polyvinyl chloride, respectively.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots recovery from waste streams of hydrothermal process

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Low-carbon Energy and Power Engineering, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, 221116, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Low-carbon Energy and Power Engineering
China University of Mining and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Low-carbon Energy and Power Engineering, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, 221116, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control
College of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Waste streams from hydrothermal process typically contain high levels of aldehydes, volatile fatty acids, phenols, 5-hydroxymethyl furfural, and some dissolved organic compounds that could contaminate water bodies and soils. To address the problems of high organic content and high disposal costs in waste streams generated in hydrothermal process. This work proposes a process for the recovery and extraction of carbon dots (CDs) from hydrothermal waste streams and a new idea for the preparation of CDs by hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) of high-ash biomass with polyvinyl chloride (PVC). The HTC of corncob (CC), corn stalk (CS), and the co-HTC of PVC with CC, CS waste streams were used as raw materials for the extraction and recovery of CDs. The results showed that the CDs obtained from the HTC of CC, CS (CC-CDs, CS-CDs) were more homogeneous in particle size and distribution and had more hydrophilic functional groups on the surface. Whereas the CDs obtained from the co-HTC of PVC with CC, CS (PCC-CDs, PCS-CDs) were of different particle sizes and had an aggregated distribution. In addition, the incorporation of PVC improved the excitation wavelength dependence of CDs. The relative percentage of Si2p in the PCS-CDs is much higher than that in the other CDs, with higher fluorescence quantum yields. Based on these results, the feasibility of the process for extracting CDs from hydrothermal waste streams was evaluated, providing theoretical guidance for extracting valuable products from hydrothermal waste streams and compensating the cost of wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) decomposes organic substances by treating materials in liquid at a specific temperature and pressure in either a subcritical or supercritical state [1]. It has been regarded as a promising process to efficiently produce clean, solid biofuels. Compared with thermochemical conversion means like pyrolysis and gasification, HTC operates in milder conditions and is not restricted by feedstock moisture content [2]. Ning et al. [3] studied the HTC of polyvinyl chloride (PVC) at 250 °C to obtain hydrochar with a rich pore structure and better combustion behavior. In our previous work [4], co-hydrothermal carbonization (co-HTC) of lignocellulosic biomass with PVC promoted the removal of chlorine and alkali metals. The hydrochar obtained by co-HTC exhibited favorable combustion properties, such as higher carbon content and oxygenated functional group content, elevated calorific value, and reduced ash content [5].

Many organics and organic carbon were hydrolyzed and dissolved in the liquid phase. This led to high levels of organic carbon and numerous toxic compounds in waste streams, which hinder the large-scale application of HTC [6–9]. The waste streams typically contain high levels of volatile fatty acids, phenols, aldehydes, 5-hydroxymethyl furfural, furfural, and other dissolved organic compounds [10]. The direct discharge of waste streams will contaminate water bodies and soils. Anaerobic digestion [11], wet air oxidation [9], and membrane distillation [12] are not appropriate for the treatment of hydrothermal waste streams because of their high cost, high chemical oxygen demand (COD), and low biochemical oxygen demand. Consequently, a method of wastewater treatment must be found that is both economical and effective.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely utilized in chemical sense [13], bio-sense [14], bioimaging [15], nano-medicine [16], photocatalysis [17], and electrocatalysis [18] due to their high solubility and chemical stability, excellent fluorescence properties, good optical properties, good biocompatibility, UV absorption, and photoelectric effect. Based on various carbon sources, the production techniques for CDs are categorized into “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches. The “top-down” approach entails breaking down a large carbon material into tiny CDs through physical or chemical exfoliation. The high cost and non-renewable nature of the precursors usually limit this approach. The “bottom-up” approach involves the chemical synthesis of CDs from small organic molecules. Hydrothermal synthesis is the most widely used “bottom-up” approach for preparing CDs and is well-liked due to its straightforward operation. During the hydrothermal process, organic molecules and/or polymers were combined at relatively high temperatures to form a carbon-inoculated core, which was then grown into CDs [19]. Extensive studies have recently focused on generating CDs from various biomass sources, including corn stalk (CS) [20], corncob (CC) [21], wheat straw [22], banana peel [23], and bagasse [24]. Moreover, polymer waste [25], plastic bags, and bottles [26], etc., have also been used to prepare CDs successfully. These studies augur well for the potential extraction of CDs from hydrothermal waste streams of organic matter, which can reduce the chemical oxygen demand in waste liquid. Meanwhile, the retrieval of CDs can offset the expenses associated with treating the waste liquid, thereby enhancing the economic viability of the HTC process and facilitating its widespread adoption. Most of the existing studies are carried out with the main purpose of CDs synthesis. They are usually performed at mild temperatures and for long periods to obtain CDs with a more uniform size and superior fluorescence properties. Nonetheless, as far as we know, no research has been conducted to assess the practicality of extracting CDs from different waste streams produced by the HTC process. This study explored the potential for extracting CDs from HTC waste streams under conditions conducive to hydrochar production. Four waste streams from HTC of CC, CS, and co-HTC of PVC with CC, and CS, were utilized as raw materials to recover CDs. Four CDs were successfully abstracted from these streams and tested for surface, physicochemical, and optical properties. Possible CDs formation mechanism was discussed according to these characterizations. This research could provide a fresh viewpoint on effectively managing waste byproducts from the HTC procedure.

Biomass wastes, including CC and CS, were collected from a local farmland (Xuzhou, China). CC exhibited the greatest amount of hemicellulose, whereas CS contained the most cellulose content [27–29]. All samples were dried in an electronic oven at 105 ± 5 °C until constant weight. Subsequently, each sample was ground and filtered to achieve a particle size of 40 mesh (approximately 0.425 mm). Shanghai Yangli Electromechanical Technology Co., Ltd. Provided us with PVC resin powder (approximately 110–120 mesh).

Our previous work provides detailed HTC operating procedures [30]. In summary, 250 g of raw substances (CC, CS, mixtures of CC, CS with PVC at a mass ratio of 9 to 1) were thoroughly mixed with 2.5 L of deionized water in a 5 L quartz tube. The combined samples were subsequently HTC in a volume of 5 L of autoclave manufactured by Yantai Songling Chemical Equipment Co., Ltd., China. The autoclave was heated to a temperature of 260 °C and then maintained for 30 min. The stirring rate was 100 rpm. After the reaction, the sample was cooled with circulating water, and the electronic heater was turned off. Gas was extracted from the reactor using a gas sample bag once the temperature fell below 30 °C. The vacuum filtration apparatus was used to filter the mixture to obtain hydrochar and waste streams. By filtering waste streams through a microporous membrane of 0.22 μm, insoluble contaminants were removed and waste streams was collected. The filtered solution containing CDs was concentrated by rotary evaporation. After concentration, waste streams was dialyzed for 72 h in a dialysis bag of 500 Da molecular weight cut-off (MWCO). To enhance dialysis efficiency, the deionized water was replaced every 6 h. The 0.22 μm microporous membrane was used to filter waste streams again after dialysis. The resultant solution was freeze-dried at −60 °C in a vacuum freeze-dryer to a consistent weight to obtain CDs powder. The obtained CDs were stored in a desiccator for further experiments. Samples denoted as “CC-CDs” and “CS-CDs” were synthesized by the HTC of CC and CS, respectively. Samples denoted as “PCC-CDs” and “PCS-CDs” were synthesized by co-HTC of PVC with CC, and CS, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA), both operating at 200 kV, were utilized to examine the morphological characteristics of CDs. The distance between lattice points was determined from HR-TEM images utilizing Gatan Digital Micrograph software (Gatan, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed on an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Smart Lab SE, Japan) using Cu Ka radiation at a scan rate of 0.020 2θ s−1. The accelerated voltage and applied current were 40 kV and 40 mA, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+, USA) was used to examine the surface chemistry of CDs. The binding energy was calibrated using the C1s peak at 284.6 eV. Surface functional groups of CDs were examined using fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) with a Thermo Scientific™ Nicolet™ IS5 from the USA.

The Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were scanned and recorded at 1 nm intervals within the 800-200 nm range using the UV-1800PC spectrophotometer from Shanghai Mapada Instruments Co., Ltd., China. A fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS5, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) was used to study the photoluminescence (PL) properties.

The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was measured using a standard reference technique. A 0.1 mol/L sulfuric acid solution containing quinine sulfate (
ψ x
= 0.54) was chosen as the standard reference. As a result of the equation below, the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was calculated.
ψ x
=
ψ s
I x
I s
A s
A x
η x 2
η s 2
where x and s represent the test substance and reference, respectively, ψ is the fluorescence quantum yield. η represents the refractive index (1.33 for aqueous solutions). A represents the absorbance at the optimum excitation wavelength. I is the integrated fluorescence intensity in the fluorescence emission spectrum.

The size, morphology, and distribution of CDs were analyzed by TEM and HR-TEM. Fig. 1 shows TEM, HR-TEM, and particle size distribution statistics of different CDs. According to Fig. 1 (a2-d2), CC-CDs, CS-CDs, PCC-CDs, and PCS-CDs exhibit clear lattice stripes. The lattice spacing was of 0.20, 0.21, 0.28, and 0.30 nm, respectively. The lattice spacings of 0.20 and 0.21 nm correspond to the (100) diffraction plane of graphite carbon, while the lattice spacings of 0.28 and 0.30 nm correspond to the (002) diffraction plane of a graphite carbon, indicating that the core of CDs is composed of graphite structure. This indicated that the core of the CDs is composed of a graphite structure [31,32]. As shown in Fig. 1 (a3-d3), the average particle sizes of CC-CDs, CS-CDs, PCC-CDs, and PCS-CDs were 2.69, 3.03, 14.82, and 12.14 nm, respectively. The distribution of CDs generated by HTC and co-HTC differs significantly. The distribution of CC-CDs and CS-CDs shown in Fig. 1 (a1 and b1) is more dispersed. PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs shown in Fig. 1 (c1 and d1) showed obvious agglomeration. As shown in Fig. S1, PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs are agglomerated and coated inside spherical structures. This indicates that carbon sources and hydrothermal conditions contribute greatly in controlling the sizes and surface morphologies of CDs. Most of the studies conclude that CDs are mainly formed by the hydrolysis of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin to generate small-molecule precursors of organic matter during HTC process, followed by condensation. The particle sizes of PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs are larger than those of CC-CDs and CS-CDs. This may be due to the conversion of Cl in PVC to HCl as a result of substitution and elimination reactions during the co-HTC process [33], which accelerated the polymerization of PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs to form H+-attracted macromolecules [34]. The acidic environment promotes the hydrolysis of lignin by breaking ether bonds, which leads to the formation of more CDs precursors during the co-HTC. During the co-HTC process, the encapsulation of PVC in the molten state causes the CDs to agglomerate.

The crystal structures of these four types of CDs (CC-CDs, CS-CDs, PCC-CDs, and PCS-CDs) were determined using XRD patterns. As shown in Fig. 2 , broad diffraction peaks appeared at 22.24°, 22.80°, 22.56°, and 22.74° for CC-CDs, CS-CDs, PCC-CDs, and PCS-CDs, respectively. In particular, the diffraction peak at 22.24° corresponds to a crystal spacing of 0.40 nm, while the diffraction peaks at 22.80°, 22.56°, and 22.74°, all correspond to a crystal spacing of 0.39 nm. This indicates that these CDs have amorphous structures, consistent with findings from previous reports [35,36].

The surface of CDs was analyzed using FTIR to determine the presence of functional groups. According to Fig. 3 , CC-CDs, CS-CDs, PCC-CDs, and PCS-CDs have the same major absorption peaks, indicating that their surfaces contain the same functional groups. Among them, the O-H stretching vibration explains the peak at 3395 cm−1. The stretching vibration of CH2 accounts for the peak at 2924 cm−1 [37]. The vibrational absorption band of 1705 cm−1 corresponds to the presence of C=O [1]. Three sets of absorption peaks at 1394-1620 cm−1 correspond to the aromatic skeleton vibrations. The absorption bands of PCS-CDs at 1110 cm−1, 800 cm−1, and 470 cm−1 correspond to Si-O-Si (antisymmetric stretching vibration), Si-O-Si (symmetric stretching vibration), and Si-O (bending vibration), respectively [38]. The peak at 960 cm−1 is attributed to the Si-OH stretching vibration [39].

Compared with PCS-CDs and PCC-CDs, the O-H bond and C=O bond of CS-CDs and CC-CDs are stronger. This is due to the elimination and substitution dechlorination that occur during co-HTC, which deplete the O-H functional group [4]. The reduction of hydrophilic groups O-H also leads to poorer solubility of PCS-CDs and PCC-CDs in water. During the experiments, it was found that CC-CDs and CS-CDs were rapidly dissolved in deionized water, while PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs needed to be sonicated to be fully dissolved. The vibrational absorption of the aromatic ring backbone of CS-CDs and CC-CDs was stronger than that of PCS-CDs and PCC-CDs. It can be inferred that the structure of CS-CDs and CC-CDs is more stable. The Si-O-Si (antisymmetric stretching vibration) at 1110 cm−1 for PCS-CDs is significantly stronger than that for the remaining three CDs. This is attributed to the higher content of silica being retained during the co-HTC process. We speculated that a composite of carbon and silica was formed during the co-HTC of PVC and CS.

In this section, the evolution of C and O functional groups will be semi-quantitatively studied by XPS to further analyze the properties of four CDs. CDs are mainly composed of four elements: C, O, N, and Si. From Table 1 , it can be concluded that the proportion of O and C atoms in CC-CDs, CS-CDs, and PCC-CDs is similar. Among them, C1s accounted for 65.71–73.58 %, and O1s accounted for 24.42–30.52 %. This indicates that the carbonization and oxidation process of the three CDs are similar. In particular, the content of O1s in PCS-CDs far exceeded that of C1s, which were 56.35 % and 20.99 %, respectively. As a result, PCS-CDs have a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on their surfaces. In addition, the content of N1s in the four CDs was 0.76–1.50 %. The Si2p content of the four CDs ranged from 0.44 to 21.81 %, which may be concerned with the biomass composition and the reaction process. Due to the presence of PVC in the co-HTC process, the content of Cl2p in CDs has attracted attention, ranging from 0.06–0.10 %. Based on the change in the content of Cl2p, we believe that no doping of elemental Cl occurs by this process. The high-resolution C1s spectra of four CDs in Fig. 4 (b, e, h, and k) were deconvoluted into three main peaks: C-C/C = C (284.69–284.74 eV), C-O (286.06–286.41 eV), and C = O (288.57–288.78 eV). For CC-CDs, CS-CDs, and PCC-CDs, the high-resolution spectra of O1s in Fig. 4 (c, f, and i) show three peaks of C=O (531.14–531.24 eV), O-H (532.12–532.30 eV), and C-O (533.17–533.52 eV). The high-resolution O1s spectra of PCS-CDs (Fig. 4l) mainly include O-H (530.70 eV), SiO2 (532.65 eV), and C-O (534.56 eV). In addition, the high-resolution Si2p spectra of the PCS-CDs (Fig. S2) show a single peak at 103.02 eV produced by SiO2 and a peak at 104.10 eV attributed to Si-O-C.

In response to the presence of high levels of Si in PCS-CDs, we attribute this to the feedstock and the reaction process. Corn is one of the active Si uptake species, and CS accumulates large amounts of SiO2 during growth [40]. At the same time, SiO2 accounts for the highest proportion of CS ash [41]. As can be seen from Table 2 , the ash contents of CC, CS, and PVC were 1.65 %, 12.35 %, and 0 %, respectively. The ash contents of hydrochars derived from HTC of CC (HC-CC) and HTC of CS (HC-CS) were 2.5 % and 17.49 %, respectively. The ash contents of co-hydrochars derived from co-HTC of PVC and CC (HC-PCC) and co-HTC of PVC and CS (HC-PCS) were 1.03 % and 14 %, respectively. We speculate that during the co-HTC of PVC with CC and CS, the encapsulation of molten PVC caused SiO2 to attach to the surface of the CDs. This phenomenon can be confirmed by the TEM images in Fig. S1.

The optical properties of CDs play a significant role in their potential applications [42]. As a result, UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, and fluorescence quantum yields were used to investigate the optical properties of CDs. Fig. 5 and its insets show the UV–vis spectra of the four aqueous solutions of CDs and the images under different light conditions, respectively. As seen in Fig. 5, the aqueous solutions of CC-CDs and CS-CDs were pale yellow in sunlight. While the aqueous solutions of PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs were nearly colorless and transparent under sunlight. This is attributed to the liquid HCl promoting biomass hydrolysis during the co-HTC process. Small molecules (furfural, ketone, formic acid, etc.) produced by the degradation of the biomass lighten the color of the solution [43]. CDs exhibit a distinct UV absorption peak in aqueous solution. The absorption peaks at 232 nm, 264 nm, 266 nm, and 277 nm are attributed to the π-π∗ transition in the aromatic sp2 domain [37,44,45]. The peak at 363 nm is attributed to the n-π∗ transition of the C = O bond [46]. The fluorescence quantum yields of CC-CDs, CS-CDs, PCC-CDs, and PCS-CDs were 1.11 %, 0.57 %, 0.41 %, and 1.51 %, respectively. This also confirms that Si is involved in the synthesis of CDs as played earlier and that Si doping improves the fluorescence properties of CDs.

The excitation wavelength dependence of CDs is important in areas such as anti-counterfeiting and bioimaging. The excitation wavelength dependence is usually affected by the surface defects of CDs, quantum size effects, the distribution of surface states, and the relaxation phenomena of solvents [47,48]. The fluorescence intensity and excitation wavelength dependence of CDs were investigated by observing their PL spectra at excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm (in 10 nm increments). Fig. 6 shows the PL spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. The optimal excitation wavelengths for CC-CDs, CS-CDs, PCC-CDs, and PCS-CDs were 350, 350, 350, and 340 nm, respectively. The emission wavelengths observed under the optimal excitation conditions were 451, 449, 471, and 444 nm, respectively. It was found that the emission peak positions of CC-CDs and CS-CDs did not change as the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 350 nm, while the fluorescence intensity gradually increased. The emission peaks here are probably fluorescence from the surface state of the carbon dots, independent of the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength continued to increase to 400 nm, the center positions of the fluorescence emission peaks of CC-CDs and CS-CDs were red-shifted from 451 to 449 nm to 505 and 478 nm, respectively, and the fluorescence intensities gradually decreased. As the excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 320 nm, the center of the emission peak of PCC-CDs shifts from 468 to 460 nm, and the fluorescence intensity increases. When the excitation wavelength continues to increase to 400 nm, the center of the emission peak is red-shifted to 495 nm, and the fluorescence intensity increases first, then decreases. For PCS-CDs, when the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 nm to 330 nm, the center position of the emission peak was blue-shifted from 456 nm to 436 nm, and the fluorescence intensity increased. When the excitation wavelength continued to increase to 400 nm, the emission peak red-shifted to 489 nm, and the fluorescence intensity first increased and then decreased. The excitation wavelength dependence of PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs may be related to alterations in the surface states and quantum size. Due to the wide range of particle sizes of PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs, when irradiated with light of different excitation wavelengths, the smaller CDs preferentially absorb light energy and emit light of higher energy, resulting in a blue shift of the fluorescence emission spectra [49]. As the excitation wavelength is further increased, larger-sized CDs begin to absorb more light energy, resulting in a red shift in the emission wavelength, as they tend to emit the light with lower energy [50]. A larger excitation wavelength corresponds to photons at a lower energy. The smaller the size of the CDs, the more pronounced the shift of their energy bands from continuous to discrete energy levels, leading to a widening of the bandgap, which requires light of higher energy to excite the electronic transition [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the effect of PVC on the properties of CDs in co-HTC
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above study, it was found that the CDs (PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs) produced by co-HTC had an inhomogeneous particle size and aggregated distribution. It was found by XPS and FTIR characterization that the four CDs were mainly composed of C, O, N, and Si, and all of them contained abundant functional groups on their surfaces, such as O-H, C=O, and benzene rings. The reduction of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs resulted in their poor solubility. Unusually, PCS-CDs contain a large amount of Si, which is caused by the large amount of SiO2 in the CS and the reaction process. CDs (CC-CDs and CS-CDs) produced by HTC have superior fluorescence properties, as evidenced by higher fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quantum yield. However, CC-CDs and CS-CDs did not exhibit excitation wavelength dependence when the CDs were irradiated with excitation less than the optimal excitation wavelength and only exhibited excitation dependence when the excitation wavelength was greater than the optimal excitation wavelength. In contrast, PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs have excitation wavelength dependence throughout the excitation wavelength range.

The CDs are formed in three main stages: hydrolysis, polymerization, and carbonation. Hemicellulose, cellulose, and lignin were the main precursors of CDs in this study. During HTC, hemicellulose and cellulose hydrolyze at 180 °C and 200 °C, respectively, while lignin decomposes between 200 °C and 500 °C. Hemicellulose and cellulose (oligosaccharides and polysaccharides) are hydrolyzed to monosaccharide units (glucose), which are converted to (5-hydroxymethyl furfural) HMF, which is dehydrated to form an aromatic carbonaceous structure that constitutes the carbon skeleton of CDs. The hydrolysis of lignin produces phenolics, which are thought to be precursors of CDs.

During the co-HTC of PVC with biomass, Cl of PVC turns into HCl by substitution or elimination reactions [4], which increases the acidity of the hydrothermal liquid phase. In acidic environments, the hydrolysis of hemicellulose, cellulose, and lignin is more complete, producing more small molecules that polymerize and carbonize to form CDs. This is shown in the TEM image as a higher number of CDs and a larger particle size. However, PVC coats the surface of CDs during HTC, leading to a decrease in their fluorescence properties.

In this study, to address the problem of high organic content in hydrothermal process water, the effects of different biomass and added PVC on the properties of CDs were derived by recovering CDs from the HTC of CC and CS and the co-HTC of CC and CS with PVC and investigating their physicochemical and optical properties. The results of the study are as follows.
1.
Compared with PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs, CC-CDs and CS-CDs are more uniform in particle size and distribution and contain more surface functional groups, which provide superior fluorescence properties and stronger water solubility.
2.
The fluorescence quantum yields of CC-CDs, CS-CDs, PCC-CDs and PCS-CDs were 1.11 %, 0.57 %, 0.41 %, and 1.51 %, respectively.
3.
Cl in PVC was converted to HCl through substitution and elimination reactions during HTC process. By promoting the decomposition of more precursors, HCl could change the size and surface functional groups of the prepared CDs, which might improve their dependence on excitation wavelength.
4.
Si in ash participated in the synthesis of CDs during the co-HTC of CS and PVC, which improved the fluorescence properties of the synthesized CDs.

Lin Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Jing Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Siyu Zhao: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Chao Feng: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yanan Lv: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Zicong Wei: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Peitao Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xueping Song: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors state that they have no recognized financial conflicts or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research received funding from the Xuzhou Science and Technology Program (KC23356), the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control (2021KF20), the Foundation of National Natural Science Foundation of China (51906079). The above funded countries are all China.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biombioe.2024.107589.
